headlines.title: REFUSE BAIL IN KILLING
headlines.subtitle: Woman Attempts to Swallow Broken Glass After Tragedy. SHOOTING IN HOUSE. Jack Thompson Waives Preliminary Hearing on Murder Charge.
headlines.fullText: Jack Thompson, a horse trader, giving his address as Lampasas, was formally charged with murder Saturday morning, following the death late Friday of John West, 125 Maple street, from four pistol shot wounds. Thompson appeared before Justice Maben and waived examining trial, and was remanded to jail without bond. He refused to say what his defense would be. Bessie Williams, who is famous as a woman jail breaker, having escaped from the city jail ten days ago by digging out the wall with a rock, was a witness to the killing. She was in the house, 125 Maple street, Friday afternoon with West, whom she had known for several years when Thompson came in, she said in a signed statement. She had also known Thompson for a long time. Both she and West were drinking, she admitted. They quarreled, she said, and she looked up and saw some one in the doorway. The shots followed and West, who was sitting on the bed, fell back dead. Three bullets went into his chest, and a fourth struck him in the groin. Small boys followed Thompson to the Delaware bar on Main street, near Fourth. One of the boys told Policeman Akcen of his whereabouts. Thompson was leaning against the bar and Akcen grabbed a pistol away from him. Policeman Davison, who had been in a chase for Thompson, came upon the scene a few minutes later. Thompson was sent to jail. Before the killing of West, a woman in the neighborhood said Thompson came into her home and demanded that she fix him his supper. She refused, she said, and he then took out his pistol and fired two shots into the floor at her feet. Sanitation Officer Ben Dollins was the first to arrive on the scene of the shooting, riding in a Robertson ambulance. He found the body of West sprawled out on the bed, his head wrapped in a towel. Woman Eating Glass. Bessie Williams was sitting on the side of the bed. She was screaming, and was eating glass. Blood was flowing from her hand, which was cut. She had taken a glass bottle from a stand and broken it and then put the pieces in her hand. She was pounding the glass into a powder. While officers were wrapping the body of West in a sheet, the woman poured the broken glass into her hand and then put it into her mouth. She tried to swallow parts of it, but couldn't and was forced to spit it out again. West's body was sent to the Robertson's morgue. Bessie Williams was sent to jail, but she threatened to break down the cell and officers put her in the dungeon on the men's side of the jail.

headlines.title: Official of Gas Company to Take Los Angeles Place
headlines.subtitle: C. W. SEARS. O. K. Shannon Will Succeed C. W. Sears—Latter Goes to Los Angeles.
headlines.fullText: O. K. Shannon, secretary of the Fort Worth Gas Company, will become manager Sept. 1, with the departure of C. W. Sears, the present manager, for Los Angeles, where he will become manager of the Southern California Gas Company. J. L. McCown, chief accountant, will become secretary, with the change in managers, and Maurice Adams will succeed McCown as chief accountant. Sears came to Fort Worth five years ago with the introduction of natural gas. He was first superintendent and then manager for four years. Los Angeles is just introducing natural gas and it will be Sears' business to establish its use there. The change in management will mean absolutely no change in the policy of the company, according to Shannon.

headlines.title: 'JAYWALKING' CASE GOES TO COUNTY COURT
headlines.subtitle: Three Convictions Under New City Traffic Law Are Appealed.
headlines.fullText: Three cases involving violations of the city's new traffic ordinance were appealed Saturday morning. Fred Jones was charged with "cutting the corner" at Ninth and Commerce streets; J. L. Howell with leaving an automobile standing longer than five minutes and Dr. W. B. Smith with driving without lights. All were found guilty in the municipal court. M. H. Landers, and C. E. Rogers, charged with speeding and fined $5 and costs each, also appealed their cases.

headlines.title: ARSON PROBE URGED UPON GRAND JURY
headlines.fullText: Sunday laws, liquor laws, pistol "toting" and the sale of cocaine were especially emphasised in a charge delivered by District Judge R. H. Buck of the Forty-eighth district court to the grand jury impaneled in that court Saturday morning. In connection with references to the crime of arson, the court spoke of several fires which have occurred here recently, particular reference being made to one in Polytechnic during the last week, which the court said was attributed to burglars. The necessity for having the county attorney or one of his assistants with the grand jury was emphasized in the charge. Judge Buck requested that in case there should be any investigation of the county attorney's office, which would render it undesirable to have the county attorney present, to report that fact to the court, so that he could name special counsel for such an emergency. The jury as impaneled is composed of Harwood James, J. J. Lewis, H. L. Agee, Will Poe, J. B. Sprinkle of Fort Worth, S. A. Jernigan of Euless, E. P. Van Zandt of Saginaw, T. R. Wallace and J. D. McKnight of Arlington and J. E. Phillips of Smithfield, with Harwood as foreman. Judge Buck departed from the usual custom by naming the bailiffs for the grand jury himself, instead of leaving it to the sheriff. He named Mitchel Higgins, R. S. Ladd and J. I. Sandsberry.

headlines.title: CITIZENS ASK GRAVELING OF FIVE STREETS
headlines.subtitle: Petition for Paving of Lower Houston Street to Be Presented.
headlines.fullText: Petitions were filed with the city commission Saturday by property owners asking that the following streets be graveled: May from Capps to Bowie, Cantey from Jennings to St. Louis, Lowdon from Jennings to St. Louis and Bowie from Jennings avenue to the railroad. A petition asking that lower Houston from Tenth to East Front be paved was also said to be ready to be filed with the commission. Work of paving Terrell avenue from the east line of Henderson street to the city limits was begun ten days ago and discontinued on account of rains, will be resumed Monday. The pavement will be of vertical fibre brick on a concrete base. The right-of-way is twenty-four feet. The estimated cost is $4,000. Two parts of Hemphill paving are under way. The south end, from the south line of Berry street to the city limits, a distance of 2,500 feet, is nearing completion. The pavement is bitulithic. The estimated cost is $25,000. The other end, from Pennsylvania to the railroad, 1,950 feet, will cost $25,271. The average width is forty feet. The city has at present no defense system to care for the storm water at Hemphill and Daggett has been commenced, and work will begin Monday on a storm sewer on Main street from the I. & G. N. tracks to Vickery boulevard. The storm sewer system in Jennings avenue has been completed, according to Engineer Francis Wagner of the city engineering department.

headlines.title: Dishrag Left on Stove Causes House to Burn
headlines.fullText: A dishrag which was left on the kitchen stove to dry caused the destruction of the home of J. A. Durken, 809 Porter street, at 2 o'clock Saturday morning. The rag caught fire and spread the flame to the kitchen wall. The loss to the house and contents was $1,500. J. W. Draughon was the owner of the house. Some insurance was carried on the contents.

headlines.title: FUNERAL OF DR. BOBO TO BE HELD SUNDAY
headlines.subtitle: Body of Physician Will Reach Here on Sunday Morning From Topeka.
headlines.fullText: Relatives left Topeka, Kan., Saturday with the body of Dr. J. E. Bobo, who died there Friday. They will reach Fort Worth Sunday at 8 a. m. over the Santa Fe. The body will be taken first to the home of Mrs. Frank Edelbrock at 1614 Jennings avenue, and those who wish to attend the funeral at Bedford will leave her residence in automobiles at 12:20. They will take either the Arlington road or the Wautauga road. Friends in Fort Worth desire to go to Bedford in automobiles can see Tom Cannon, who will take them to Bedford. The funeral will be at the Bedford Christian Church at 2 p. m. The pallbearers will be Dr. W. M. Trimble, Lee M. Hammond, J. E. Valentine, I. T. Valentine, Will Day and Tom Cannon.

headlines.title: LORD QUITS AS RESERVOIR SUPT. SEPT. 1
headlines.subtitle: Builder of Dam Possible Candidate for Waterworks Commissioner.
headlines.fullText: J. C. Lord, who for fifteen months past has been superintendent of construction of the Lake Worth reservoir, tendered his resignation to the city commissioners Saturday. "The reservoir stands as a monument to the progressiveness of a wonderful city," said Lord. The resignation becomes effective Sept. 1. It is said that Lord will be a possible candidate for the office of waterworks commissioner. His salary as superintendent of construction was the same as that of a commissioner, $3,000 per annum.

headlines.title: YOUNG ASKS RELEASE ON HABEAS CORPUS
headlines.subtitle: Held to Grand Jury, He Claims Hearing Failed to Show Offense
headlines.fullText: J. P. Young, who was held to the grand jury on a charge of swindling, after his hearing Friday before Justice Maben, began habeas corpus proceedings in the Forty-eighth district court Saturday to secure his release on the ground that the testimony produced at the preliminary hearing did not show the commission of any offense. Judge Buck set the case for Saturday afternoon because of the delay in empaneling and charging the grand jury. J. P. Young's bond was fixed at $500 in each of two cases.

headlines.title: WATER COMMISSIONER WARNED ABOUT FISH
headlines.subtitle: Failure to Provide Ladders at Dams Said to Be Violation of Law.
headlines.fullText: Warning that the city water commissioner has failed to comply with a certain section of the penal code and that prosecution may follow in the event action is not taken at once was filed with City Secretary Harwood Saturday in a letter addressed to Commissioner L. H. Blanke and signed by County Judge Jesse Brown. The warning calls attention to the law which provides that all persons who erect dams across any stream must provide ladders so that at all seasons of the year fish may ascend above the dams. Failure to comply with the law, the letter points out, carries a penalty of from $25 to $100 fine. He requests that Blanke at once make provisions for fish ladders at dams one, two and three on the Clear Fork river and at Lake Worth, in conjunction with Deputy State Game Warden J. H. Thompson of Fort Worth.

headlines.title: NEW TRAFFIC LAW BRINGS CITY $3,489
headlines.subtitle: Tags for Autos Number 1,451 and Licenses for Drivers Total 1,612.
headlines.fullText: The city has been enriched to the extent of $3,489.50 by reason of the new traffic law, according to figures furnished Saturday by Charles W. Newby, clerk in the city tax collector's office. The number of tags issued for automobiles was 1,451 and licenses for drivers for the same autos, 1,612. The vehicle licenses issued totaled 85. The auto tag and licenses were issued at $1 each. The vehicle licenses cost 50 cents each.

headlines.title: MOTORCYCLIST COLLIDES WITH AUTO; INJURED
headlines.fullText: Walter Helmkamp, employed by the Cromer Cycle Company, was seriously injured late Saturday afternoon when his motorcycle collided with an automobile driven by Dr. D. Bozeman, resident physician at the Arlington Heights sanitarium. Both of Helmkamp's legs were broken. The accident occurred at the point where the road to the Arlington Heights sanitarium branches from the Arlington Heights road. Dr. Wilmer Allison and his wife were in the automobile.

headlines.title: ALLEGED FUGITIVE IDENTIFIED HERE
headlines.subtitle: Arrested In Oak Cliff Said to Be Luke Ponder, Convicted of Highway Robbery.
headlines.fullText: A man arrested in Oak Cliff and brought here Friday night for identification was identified Saturday by Fort Worth officers as Luke Ponder, who was convicted of highway robbery in the district court here in 1909 and escaped from the penitentiary about three years ago. Ponder was raised near Keller and several people from there were brought to Fort Worth to assist in the identification. The prisoner denies that he is Ponder, but there are several people who have identified him.

headlines.title: LICENSE PROVIDED FOR CIRCUS PARADES
headlines.subtitle: Ordinance Passed Regulating Amusements at Discretion of Mayor.
headlines.fullText: Circus owners who have the fond hope of reaping a harvest by reason of crowds in attendance at the Fat Stock Show in October, may feel their prospects slipping by reason of an ordinance passed by the city commission Saturday. The ordinance provides that no permit shall be issued for a parade of a circus without a fee of $25 and that the license shall be issued or refused at the discretion of the mayor. It was pointed out Saturday by members of the commission that the mayor might refuse to accept the fee and thereby preclude the possibility of a parade.
